caption: Breaking down how the FBI is using your data for surveillance
audio_track_actual: Single male narrator (Channel 5 / Andrew Callaghan house style) over a sequence of archival news clips, social-media screenshots, and motion-graphic overlays.
video_overlay_text_observed: On-screen text callouts of named entities — Oracle, the FBI, Palantir, the Pentagon — interspersed with archival hearing-room footage and a sign-off graphic identifying the Channel 5 attribution.
audio_content_summary: The narrator describes two interlocking facts: (1) On March 18, 2026, FBI Director Kash Patel testified under oath before the Senate Intelligence Committee that the Bureau purchases commercially available data on Americans from private data brokers, declining a request from Senator Ron Wyden to commit to stopping the practice. (2) Palantir Technologies' Maven Smart System has been formalized as a Department of Defense Program of Record, with the Maven contract ceiling raised from $480 million (2024) to approximately $1.3 billion (2025), alongside a separate Army enterprise framework agreement worth up to $10 billion over a decade. Both anchor events are documented in the linked sources below. The video's editorial proposition is that these two threads — a federal agency openly purchasing commercial data on U.S. persons, and a single AI vendor becoming the default DOD analytical platform — together describe the architecture of a domestic surveillance system. The factual scaffolding for that proposition is in the linked records.
implied_frame: The video frames Patel's testimony and the Palantir program-of-record designation as components of the same architecture rather than as separate news items. The shared infrastructure question — who holds the data, who runs the analysis, and what oversight regime governs both — is the editorial throughline.
capture_method: Headless browser recording of the in-page <video> element via MediaRecorder over captureStream(), viewport 1280x800.
